wain_guo.name: Wain Guo
wain_guo.summary:
Hailing from Missouri, Wain Guo knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 7 entries across 4 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

What makes Wain especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

In doubles, Wain has formed effective partnerships:
  • Katherine Y Li (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD

Wain is peaking at the right time in 2022-2023, with 1 title across 2 tournaments — a clear sign that Wain is still ascending.
wain_guo.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Wain Guo, from Missouri, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 1 win across 7 entries in 4 tournaments. That is a 14% conversion rate, which means roughly 9 out of every 10 times Wain shows up to a tournament, Wain pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Wain saga began at 2022 Midwest CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2022 Egret Midwest CRC in XD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Wain's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Wain plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Wain is 0-for-4. Zero titles in 4 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Wain and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Wain needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Wain's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Wain has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Wain showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U19, which means Wain traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 Midwest ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Wain entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Wain paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Wain's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 1 title shine bright, but they are surrounded by 6 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Wain is a player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. Will Wain figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Wain's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

wanhan_sun.name: Wanhan Sun
wanhan_sun.summary:
Representing Illinois, Wanhan Sun keeps punching through to the quarterfinals, doing so 3 times, currently competing at the U19 level, building a strong foundation across 4 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

Wanhan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Wanhan has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
wanhan_sun.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Wanhan Sun, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 8 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Wanhan is right on schedule. It all started at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC, and so far, the only thing Wanhan has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Wanhan saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Wanhan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 8 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Wanhan has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Wanhan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U19, which means Wanhan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Wanhan entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Wanhan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Wanhan keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Wanhan has survived 2 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Wanhan knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

warren_wenxuan_huang.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Warren Wenxuan Huang, from Illinois, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 11 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 11-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Warren's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Warren keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Warren saga began at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Can we talk about Warren and Boys' Singles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 2 finals, 2 losses. Warren can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Boys' Singles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Warren still cannot reach it.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Warren reaches a final. Warren has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2026 Play N Thrive MW CRC, where Warren entered 2 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U13, BD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. And remember: Warren has ZERO titles. None. 3 times in the final, zero trophies to show for it. This is the most committed bridesmaid in the history of junior badminton.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Warren plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 11 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Warren has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Jingyang Guo has emotionally recovered.

Warren has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Warren has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Warren has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Warren showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U13, which means Warren traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Warren entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Warren paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Warren is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Warren wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Wayne is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
wayne_chien.roast:
Allow us to introduce Wayne Chien, from New York, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Wayne has 72 career entries across 27 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 1% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Wayne saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13) with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2025 Capital NE OLC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Wayne's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Wayne would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Wayne has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Wayne has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Wayne's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Wayne plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Wayne is 0-for-27. Zero titles in 27 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Wayne and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Wayne needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Wayne has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Wayne's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Jonathan Cai: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Jonathan Cai has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Mrugakshi Kulkarni has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Wayne's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Wayne has shown up 5 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Wayne has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Wayne is not just losing locally — Wayne is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 1% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chien family travels cross-country so Wayne can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Wayne has 42 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Wayne showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Wayne traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Wayne entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Wayne paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title, 72 entries, and 71 that ended without a trophy. A player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the moments of brilliance — Wayne looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Wayne. We will be watching.

weirui_yu.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Weirui Yu, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 19 events across 8 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 19-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Weirui's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Weirui keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Weirui saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 2 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Weirui would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 5 times Weirui has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 5 times Weirui has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Weirui's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Weirui plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 19 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Weirui has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Jasper Chen: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Jasper Chen has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U17 — we hope Jasper Chen has emotionally recovered.

Weirui has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Weirui has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Weirui showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U17, which means Weirui traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Weirui entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Weirui paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Weirui keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Weirui has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Weirui, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

Wendy has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Wendy has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

wendy_li.roast:
Step right up and meet Wendy Li, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 33 career entries in 13 tournaments, Wendy has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 32 entries where Wendy drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 33 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Wendy saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Wendy reaches a final. Wendy has reached the finals 2 times — at 2022 SoCal CRC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Wendy has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Wendy plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 33 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Wendy has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Wendy's texts. The go-to partner is Ethan Wu (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13 — we hope Ethan Wu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Wendy entered 3 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Wendy's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Wendy and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Wendy has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Wendy is not just losing locally — Wendy is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Li family travels cross-country so Wendy can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Wendy has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Wendy showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13, which means Wendy traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Wendy entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Wendy paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Wendy keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Wendy has survived 2 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Wendy knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, Wendy changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

wesley_deheck.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Wesley Deheck, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 14 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 14-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Wesley's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Wesley keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Wesley saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Wesley plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Wesley has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Wesley showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Wesley traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Wesley entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Wesley paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Wesley keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Wesley has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Wesley, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

weslie_chen.name: Weslie Chen
weslie_chen.summary:
Hailing from California, Weslie Chen is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (BD U19) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 6 career titles across 15 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

Weslie is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #4, 6 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (BD U19, 2024) • U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U19 BD, U17 BD) • Junior Nationals champion (BD U19) • 3 ORC titles.

Weslie has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Annabelle Zhang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (6 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Isaac Yang (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (5 total events in BD/U19, across 2 seasons)
  • Kyle Wang (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 4 titles, building serious momentum (5 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)

The 2023-2024 season has been outstanding, with 6 podium finishes across 4 tournaments. Weslie is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Weslie has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Weslie has competed across 4 different regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 6 titles, Weslie is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
weslie_chen.roast:
Allow us to introduce Weslie Chen, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Weslie is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (BD U19), which means Weslie has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Weslie's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 43 career entries to discuss and a 14% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Weslie saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2022 NW ORC in BD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Weslie's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Take away Boys' Doubles and Weslie has never won anything. Not once. 30 entries across other disciplines, zero titles. Weslie is essentially a one-club golfer who showed up to a tournament with 18 holes and one iron.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Weslie experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Weslie has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Weslie does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Weslie has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 6 wins means Weslie is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Weslie plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Weslie is 0-for-11. Zero titles in 11 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Weslie and suddenly the wins start appearing — 6 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Weslie needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Weslie has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Weslie's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Annabelle Zhang: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Annabelle Zhang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U19 — we hope Annabelle Zhang has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Weslie entered 9 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Weslie's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Weslie and said 'nah.'

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Weslie is not just losing locally — Weslie is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 14% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chen family travels cross-country so Weslie can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Weslie has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Weslie showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U19, which means Weslie traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Weslie entered 3 events with results ranging from 1st to 33rd. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Weslie paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 6 titles, 43 entries, and 37 that ended without a trophy. A player who treats championship matches like optional practice sessions. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the moments of brilliance — Weslie looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Weslie. We will be watching.

william_chen.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: William Chen, from Massachusetts, continues to exist, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. William has 34 career entries across 17 tournaments and has managed to win 3 of them — a 9% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The William saga began at 2023 MassBad NE OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2025 Peak Sports South OLC in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from William's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 15 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. William plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, William can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. William has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers William's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U15 — we hope Lucas Liu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of William's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, William has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse William of is playing it safe. William has entered older age groups at 6 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means William has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 3 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and William has built a permanent residence on that line. William has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. William has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where William showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means William traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where William entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying William paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 3 titles, 34 entries, and 31 that ended without a trophy. A player who changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the moments of brilliance — William looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, William. We will be watching.

william_geng.name: William Geng
william_geng.summary:
A Washington competitor, William Geng is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (XD U15, BD U15) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 16 career titles across 30 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

William is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes William especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 2 singles titles, 5 doubles titles, 8 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (XD U15, BD U15, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 XD, U13 BD, U15 BD, U15 BS) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (XD U15, BD U15) • 4 ORC titles.

Beyond the trophies, the numbers stand out: William boasts a biggest win margin of 33 points, a career +845 point differential, a clutch 71% win rate in three-game matches (12/17).

William has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 16 different players across the career:
  • Joey Liu (current primary partner) — 13 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (13 total events in BD/U15, across 2 seasons)
  • Erica Chen Lin (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (4 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Stephanie Jiayi Song (current partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 4 titles across 5 events in XD, spanning 4 seasons

A veteran of the junior circuit, William has competed across U11, U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

William has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 15 seeded entries across the career, William consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 16 titles, William has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
william_geng.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about William Geng, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: William is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (XD U15, BD U15), which means William has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and William's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 88 career entries to discuss and a 18% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The William saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from William's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 8 titles in 25 entries — a stretch where William actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 14 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

BS U17 used to be William's personal ATM — just show up and collect the gold. That was the 2024 OBA NW OLC era. Now William is grinding for a top-9 finish in the same event. The shuttle giveth, and the shuttle taketh away.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when William reaches a final. William has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, William does own 16 actual titles, so it is not like William has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 16 wins means William is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. William plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 5 doubles, 8 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, William can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. William has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers William's texts. The most successful partnership is with Joey Liu — 13 events, 3 titles — and honestly, Joey Liu deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Joey Liu a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Nathaniel Volfbeyn has emotionally recovered.

One thing nobody can accuse William of is playing it safe. William has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means William has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 16 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and William has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because William is not just losing locally — William is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 18% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Geng family travels cross-country so William can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. William has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where William showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NW ORC in BS U13, which means William traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 South ORC in BD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where William entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying William paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The William story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 16 titles across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who living proof that past performance does not guarantee future results. But every season William comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, William, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where William entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying William paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, William has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — William knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, William the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Doubles, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

William has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. wufan_zhang.name: Wufan Zhang
wufan_zhang.summary:
Representing Texas, Wufan Zhang has tasted victory multiple times, currently competing at the U19 level, capturing 2 titles across 11 tournaments and 28 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Wufan keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Notably, Wufan has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Wufan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 7 different doubles partners over the years, Wufan adapts to anyone:
  • Scarlett Yu — a trusted U15/XD partner (6 events)
  • Napat Srisalaipaisal — 6 events, 1 title together in BD/U15

A veteran of the junior circuit, Wufan has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Wufan has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Wufan is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
wufan_zhang.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Wufan Zhang, from Texas, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 28 career entries in 11 tournaments, Wufan has 2 titles — a 7% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 13 entries where Wufan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 28 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Wufan saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 7th. The first title would not come until 2022 South Houston CRC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Wufan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Wufan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Wufan is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Wufan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Wufan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Wufan has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Scarlett Yu: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Scarlett Yu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U15 — we hope Napat Srisalaipaisal has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Wufan's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Wufan entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Wufan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Wufan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Wufan has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Wufan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Wufan is not just losing locally — Wufan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Wufan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Wufan has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Wufan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U15, which means Wufan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Wufan entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Wufan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles, 28 entries, and 26 that ended without a trophy. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the moments of brilliance — Wufan looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Wufan. We will be watching.
